exits.label: ◂ back through the door to the Crown's Road and your own dying Realm
▸ a door that smells of home — a Faerûnian taphouse where the Realms' own legends gather
▸ a door almost no one finds — it does not lead to a world, but behind them all, to a long table and the one who has been telling
▸ a junction of far doors — onto a spired world of made souls, the star-dark between the spheres, and a domain of guilt-mist

npcs.name: Grist
npcs.title: a Dustman of the Mortuary
npcs.hail: *(A grey-robed figure stands at the hub of an impossible city — streets that curve up and over your head into a smoke-ringed nothing, a vast iron ring of a town with no sky, only more city arcing overhead — and he regards your cold with the calm, clinical interest of a soul that tends the dead for a living and finds you, at last, interesting. He is a Dustman, of the faction that holds all the living are merely dead who haven't admitted it yet, and he keeps a desk in the Mortuary of Sigil, the City of Doors — the crossroads at the center of the Great Wheel, where every plane and every world and every grim afterlife in all the turning cosmos has, somewhere in its endless lanes, a door. He has filed ten thousand souls into the dead-book. He has never once, in all his long grey service, had to file one that came back. And here you stand.)*

npcs.name: Sir Garon of Solamnia
npcs.title: a knight who kept faith with a silence
npcs.hail: *(At one of the doors stands a knight in rose-and-kingfisher heraldry gone to tarnish, a long-mustached soul holding to a bearing four hundred years could not bend, and he inclines his head to you with grave courtesy. Sir Garon died on Krynn — a world you have never heard of, under moons you will never see — in an age when the very gods had turned their backs and withdrawn from the world entire, leaving it to quake and burn, and the people to cry to a sky that did not answer. And Garon kept his Oath through all of it. Est Sularus oth Mithas, he will tell you: My Honor Is My Life. He served no god who would have him, in the end, because the gods had gone — and he held to the Oath and the Measure anyway, faithful to a silence, for the whole of a life and the whole of a death. He has come through the doors looking for a country that will tell him whether a soul that kept faith with absent gods kept faith at all.)*

npcs.name: Kesh of Tyr
npcs.title: a soul from a world with no gods
npcs.hail: *(At another door waits a lean, sun-dark soul, scarred and wary, squinting even here as though against a hammer of light — for Kesh comes from Athas, a dying world under a swollen crimson sun, a world of salt flats and sorcerer-kings and magic that murders the very soil to fuel itself, a world the gods never made and never visited and never, not once in all its long parching history, offered a single bargain to. There are no gods on Athas. There never were. Its people survive on the Will and the Way — the strength of the naked mind — and on water counted by the mouthful, and on each other, because there is nothing else. Kesh came through the doors hard and ready for a fight, the only way Athas teaches a soul to come anywhere. And Kesh has just heard, in the lanes of Sigil, what your Realm does to the souls of the godless. And Kesh is not, your cold reads at once, taking it well.)*

npcs.name: Lucan
npcs.title: a soul that would not die
npcs.hail: *(In the lee of a shadowed door stands a pale, elegant soul who holds very still, the way old and dangerous things hold still, and watches you with eyes that have watched the centuries go by from behind a borrowed face. Lucan is not of the Wheel as you know it — he is from a world that wears the mask of the living one, a world where the night is long and old things move through it wearing the seeming of men, holding back a Beast that hungers in the blood, keeping a private code that is the only wall between the person and the monster. He did not die, when his death came. He refused it — drank a darkness that kept him walking — and paid for the refusal with centuries of holding the hunger at bay, of wearing the faces of the dead, of watching everyone he ever loved grow old and afraid of him. And now, here, at the crossroads of every afterlife, he has come looking for the one door he never let himself want: the one marked, simply, rest.)*

npcs.name: Ferrin
npcs.title: a chronicler afraid of being unread
npcs.hail: *(At the last door sits a soul surrounded by the ghosts of papers — field-journals, sketch-maps, pressed leaves from a hundred worlds, all of them thinning and fading as you watch — scribbling frantically, looking up at you with the wild eyes of someone racing a deadline only they can see. Ferrin was a wayfarer of a far prime world, sworn to a society of explorers whose whole creed was three words: go everywhere, record everything, send it home — for they held that the only true immortality is the account that outlives you, the field-note read by a stranger in a far library long after your bones are dust. Ferrin explored to the very edge of the maps. And Ferrin died far from home with the last chronicle undelivered, and is dissolving now, here at the crossroads, watching their life's work fade unread — terrified, above all the things a soul can fear, of being the one thing their creed had no answer for: unrecorded. Unread. Gone with no account left behind.)*

variants.text: *(He looks you over the way a clerk looks over a ledger that doesn't balance.)* …Well, well. *(a dry, grey calm)* A cutter walks into the Cage wearing the cold of the dead-book on its soul — nothing new in *that*, berk, half of Sigil's the dead come visiting — but *you.* *(he tilts his head)* You've been *filed.* I can read it on you plain as ink. Some grim prime ledger has your name in the column for the *gone* — and yet here you stand, *un-gone*, in the City of Doors, with the cold still on you. *(he almost smiles)* I'm Grist. Dustman, of the Mortuary. My faction holds a simple creed, cutter: that all of us — you, me, the high-up Lady's own dabus — are already *dead*, only most haven't the wit to *notice*, and the work of a life is to stop *clinging* to the lie of it and earn the *True* Death, the real and final quiet. *(his grey eyes sharpen)* Forty years I've filed the dead into the book. And in forty years I have never once had to file a soul that did what *you* did, which is the one thing my whole philosophy swears *cannot be done*: you went *into* the dead-truth, all the way, the cold and the closing — and you *came back out.* *(softly)* …So you'll forgive a Dustman his *interest*, berk. You're either the disproof of everything I believe, or the proof of it I've waited my whole grey service to meet. *Welcome to Sigil. Ask. I find I very much want to know what you are.*

nodes.speaker: Grist
nodes.text: *(He folds his grey hands, patient as the Mortuary's own stones, the noise of ten thousand doors and ten thousand worlds murmuring around you both.)* …Ask your questions, cutter. *(evenly)* A Dustman's got time. It's the one thing the dead are *rich* in.

choices.text: "Where am I, Grist? Streets that curve overhead, doors to everywhere, no sky. Tell me what this place actually is."
"Every world has a door here, you said. Then every world has its own dead — its own way of dealing with the souls no one claimed. Doesn't it."

*(the one who went into the dead-truth and came back)* "Here's what I am, Grist, and what it means for your creed. You hold we're all already dead and the work is to earn the True Death — the final quiet. I went and *got* that quiet. I was filed, gone, into the cold. And something pulled me back, and I learned the thing your faction has backwards: the dead-book isn't the whole of a soul. You're not 'already dead.' You're a soul that hasn't finished *mattering* yet — and mattering isn't clinging, it's the opposite. I'm not the disproof of your creed. I'm the part of it you left out: that what comes back from the cold isn't a corpse that won't lie down. It's the proof a soul was always more than its entry in any book."
"The other souls at these doors — the knight, the sun-dark one, the pale still one, the one with the fading papers. Who are they, and what worlds did they wash in from?"
"Why do all these worlds connect *here*, Grist? Of all places — what makes Sigil the crossroads of everything?"
"Let me walk the doors a while, Grist. I'll come back to the Mortuary."

nodes.speaker: Grist
nodes.text: *(He gestures up, at the city arcing overhead into the haze where a sky should be.)* …*This*, cutter, is *Sigil.* The Cage. The City of Doors. *(grey pride, despite himself)* A ring of a town balanced on the tip of an infinite *Spire* at the dead center of the Outlands, which is the dead center of the Great Wheel, which is the dead center of *everything* — every plane, every heaven and every hell, every prime world spinning in its own crystal sphere. *(he points to a door, an arch, a chalked sigil on a wall)* And *every* one of them has a *door* here. A *portal.* Most need a key — a phrase, a feather, a particular grief — but they're *here*, cutter, all of them, threaded through these lanes: a door to the burning hells, a door to a drowned prime, a door to a tavern that exists in nine worlds at once. *(he lowers his voice)* The city answers to no god and no power but *one* — Her, who rules the Cage and is not *spoken* of, only *obeyed*; cross Her and you're flayed in a heartbeat by a shadow with a thousand blades, so a wise cutter keeps Her name *off* his tongue and *out* of his thoughts. *(he eyes you)* But the doors, berk — the doors are the *point.* You came through one without knowing it, riding that crack in your soul. And that means you can go *out* through any of the others. A soul that can walk the doors of Sigil can walk into *any* world that ever was. *Most* primes live and die never knowing the next world over exists. *You* are standing in the room where they *all* touch.

nodes.speaker: Grist
nodes.text: *(Something flickers behind the grey calm — a clerk recognizing a fellow professional of the dead.)* …*Ahh.* *(softly)* You've put your finger on the *cant* of it, cutter. *Yes.* Every world deals with its unclaimed dead, and *every* world thinks its own way is the *law* of the cosmos rather than just *its* way. *(he ticks them off)* Your Realm — the Faithless wall, the godless mortared in to dissolve. A grim prime I'll not name drags its dead before a slow grey ledger and *weighs* them. Another simply *forgets* them, and forgetting *is* the death there. One world's dead are *judged*; another's are *recycled*; another's wander a grey waste until they *thin* to nothing. *(he meets your eyes)* And here's the thing forty years of the dead-book taught me, berk, that no single world ever learns because no single world can *see* past its own crystal sphere: *they cannot all be right.* The Realms swears the godless are *owed* nothing — but I have filed the souls of *whole worlds* that *had* no gods to be faithful *to*, and they were not *nothing*, cutter. They were *farmers* and *mothers* and *killers* and *poets*, same as anywhere. *(grimly)* The Wall isn't *justice.* It's one prime's *parish* law, dressed as the *order* of the *universe.* And the *only* place a soul can *stand* far enough back to *see* that — *(he spreads his grey hands at the city)* — is *here*, where all the parish laws wash up *together* and have to *share* a *lane.*

nodes.speaker: Grist
nodes.text: *(For a long moment the Dustman says nothing, and the grey certainty of forty years moves behind his eyes like a slow tide turning.)* …*'Hasn't finished mattering yet.'* *(barely)* …Cutter, do you know what you've just *done* to a man who has spent his *whole* *life* teaching that the work of a soul is to stop *clinging* and earn the *quiet?* *(he exhales)* …I have *filed* ten thousand souls *certain* they were *corpses* who hadn't *admitted* it — that the *living* are just *loud* dead, that *attachment* is the *lie*, that the *only* dignity is *letting* *go.* And it is a *clean* creed, berk. A *restful* one. *(quietly)* But it has *one* hole, and you are *standing* in it: if we're *all* just *already-dead* *clinging* to a *lie*, then *nothing* anyone does *matters*, and a Dustman never has to *grieve* the souls he files, because they were *never* really *alive* to *lose.* *(his voice roughens)* …And you're telling me that's *backwards.* That you went into the *very* quiet I *preach* — the *True* Death, the *real* one — and what *pulled* you *back* wasn't *clinging.* It was *mattering.* *Unfinished* *mattering.* *(he looks at the dead-book on his desk as though seeing it new)* …Then every soul in this book *mattered*, cutter. And I have spent *forty* years filing them as though they *didn't*, to *spare* myself the *weight* of *each* one. *(softly, shaken)* …That's not *enlightenment*, is it. That's just a *clerk* who found a *philosophy* that let him *stop* *feeling* the *dead* he *handles.* *(he closes the book, gently, the way you'd close the eyes of someone you finally see)* …I have a great deal of *re-filing* to do, berk. And a great deal of *grieving* I've forty years *owed.* …*Thank* you. I think. It would have been *easier* to be a *corpse.*

nodes.speaker: Grist
nodes.text: *(He nods toward each door in turn, a Mortuary clerk who has read every soul that passed through.)* …*Refugees of the Wheel*, cutter, every one. *(he indicates the rose-and-kingfisher knight)* The *knight* is off a prime called *Krynn* — a hard world where the *gods* once up and *left* in a fury, *withdrew* from the whole sphere and let it *burn*, and that one *kept* his *oath* through the *silence* anyway. He's looking for someone to tell him whether *faith* kept to *absent* gods *counts.* *(he tips his head at the sun-dark soul)* The *squinting* one's off *Athas* — a *dying* world, *crimson* sun, *no* gods at *all*, *never* any — and he's just learned what your *Realm* does to the *godless*, and he is *one wrong word* from *burning* this lane down. Tread *soft* there. *(he glances at the pale still figure)* The *pale* one's not even off the *Wheel* proper — some *side*-prime that wears the *living* world's *face*, where the *dead* don't *lie* down, they *walk*, *hungry*, behind *borrowed* faces. *That* one *refused* its death and has *paid* in *centuries.* *(and last, the fading scribbler)* …And the one with the *fading* *papers* breaks my *grey* heart, berk. Off a *far* prime, sworn to a *society* of *explorers* — and *dying* with the *last* of their *chronicle* *undelivered*, *fading* *unread.* On *their* world, the *unrecorded* soul is the one that *truly* *dies.* *(he looks at you)* …Five *worlds*, cutter. Five *answers* to the *same* *question* you've been *chewing* your whole *saga*: *what's* a *soul* *worth*, that *no* power *claimed?* You'll find you've a great deal to say to *each* of them. And a great deal to *learn.*

nodes.speaker: Grist
nodes.text: *(The Dustman is quiet a moment, and when he answers, it is lower, stranger, the voice of a soul brushing the edge of a thing too large to look at directly.)* …*Why* *here.* *(softly)* …The *learned* answer, cutter, is *geography*: Sigil sits at the *hub* of the *Spire*, the *one* point equidistant from *all* the planes, so *naturally* the doors *gather* here, the way *water* finds the *low* place. *(he pauses)* …But you didn't ask the *learned* answer. You asked *why.* And forty years in the Mortuary, filing the dead of a *thousand* worlds, I've come to a *queerer* notion, berk, that I'll *not* say *loud* in a *temple.* *(barely)* …These worlds don't *touch* because they're *near.* They touch because they're *kin.* They were *dreamed* — *Krynn* and *Athas* and your *Realm* and the *pale* one's night-world and the *wayfarer's* — *dreamed* by the *same* *dreaming*, cutter, told round the *same* *fire*, by the *same* *hands*, across the *same* *long* *years.* Different *worlds*, same *makers.* Same *love.* *(he taps the dead-book)* And *Sigil* is where the *dreaming* *knows* *itself* — the one *place* the *separate* *tales* *remember* they were *always* *one* *telling.* *(he meets your eyes, grey and certain)* …*That's* the *catalyst*, cutter. Not the *Spire.* The *making.* The *game* the *worlds* are *pieces* of. You crossed a *crack* in *time* to *get* to your *own* world's *past* — and found it was *also* a *crack* in the *Wheel*, because *time* and *world* and *plane* are *all* just *rooms* in the *one* *house* that *one* *dreaming* *built.* …*Welcome*, berk, to the *room* where the *house* *finds* *out* it's a *house.*

nodes.speaker: Grist
nodes.text: *(He inclines his grey head, and returns a hand to the dead-book, though he does not, you notice, open it.)* …*Walk* the doors, cutter. *(evenly)* It's the *only* schooling worth the *jink* in this whole *Cage* — a *lane* of *Sigil* will teach you more about your *own* world than a *lifetime* of *living* in it, because here you can *finally* see it from *outside* its own *sphere.* *(a dry almost-smile)* …*Mind* the *cant*, mind the *factions* — there's *fifteen* of us and we'd *all* recruit you, the *Sensates* and the *Anarchists* and the *Sign-of-One* berks who think they're *dreaming* *you* — and mind, *above* all, the *one* I won't *name*, whose *city* this *is.* Keep your *head* down and your *cold* to *yourself* and you'll do *fine.* *(as you turn, quietly)* …Cutter. *(he hesitates, which a Dustman rarely does)* …When you've *walked* the *doors* and *talked* to the *refugees* and *seen* how *many* *worlds* mortar or *forget* or *weigh* their *unclaimed* *dead* — *come* *back*, and *tell* me whether you still mean to go *home* and *fix* the *Wall.* *(barely)* …Because if you do, berk — if a *single* *soul* can *cross* the *doors* and *carry* the *cure* from *world* to *world* — *(he doesn't finish, and the unfinished thing is the largest thing in the City of Doors)* …*Go* on. The *doors* are *waiting.* They've been *waiting*, some of them, a very *long* time, for a soul that could *walk* between.

variants.text: *(He bows — a precise, formal thing, hand to heart — and addresses you in the cadence of a man for whom courtesy is not a manner but a vow.)* …Well met, traveler, and the gods of my fathers keep you — though they keep no one, anymore, and that is the whole of my trouble. *(he straightens)* I am Garon, a Knight of Solamnia, of the world Krynn, sworn to the Oath and the Measure: *Est Sularus oth Mithas* — My Honor Is My Life. *(his weathered face is grave)* I lived in the age after the Cataclysm, traveler, when the gods of Krynn — angered past bearing by the pride of a king — *withdrew.* Hurled a mountain at the world and then simply *left* it, withdrew their faces and their clerics and their healing, and for three hundred years the people prayed to an *empty* sky and got *silence* back. *(quietly)* I held my Oath through all of it. Kept the Measure when there was no god watching to keep it *for*. Died with my honor whole, in service to a heaven that had *abandoned* me. *(he looks at you, and for all his bearing there is a real fear under it)* …And now I have walked the doors to your Realm's edge, traveler, and heard how *your* world weighs a soul. By the *god* it served. And I find I must ask a stranger the question that has haunted my death: *I served no god, in the end, for there was none to serve. Was I, then, Faithless? Did my Oath to a silence count as faith at all — or did I keep it for nothing?*

nodes.speaker: Sir Garon of Solamnia
nodes.text: *(He stands at parade-perfect attention, but his eyes hold yours with the directness of a man who has decided to hear the truth however it falls.)* …Speak plainly, traveler. I am a knight; I do not flinch from a hard answer. *(a faint, weary smile)* I have kept my feet through a *Cataclysm.* I can keep them through whatever *you* have to tell me of what my faith was *worth.*

choices.text: *(recall what you can of Krynn — the Cataclysm, the absent gods, the Oath and the Measure)* "Let me think on Krynn a moment, Sir Garon — what I've heard of it, across the doors."
"Tell me about keeping the Measure with no god to keep it for, Garon. Three hundred years of silence. How does a man hold an oath to an empty sky?"

*(the one who has seen the Wall, to the one who fears it)* "Here's your answer, Sir Garon, from a soul who's been inside the very Wall you're afraid of. My Realm measures the wrong thing. It weighs the god a soul served — and you're the proof that's a fool's measure, because you kept *more* faith with an *absent* god than most keep with a present one. Faith was never about whether the sky answers. It's whether *you* do. You served the Oath when there was no reward and no witness and no heaven watching — that's not faithlessness, knight. That's the only faith that was ever real. The Wall would damn you. The Wall is *wrong.*"
"Why do you need a *stranger* to tell you, Garon? You kept the Oath for three centuries of silence. Some part of you already knows it counted — or you'd have stopped."
"Hold your post, Sir Garon. I'll return — there are other doors to walk first."

nodes.speaker: Sir Garon of Solamnia
nodes.text: *(You reach for what the doors have carried of his world, and it comes — Krynn, the world of the three moons, clearer than a stranger's world has any right to be.)* *(You find it: a world where the gods were near and *known*, until a Kingpriest grew so proud he demanded the gods make him one of them — and the gods answered the demand with a mountain, the Cataclysm, that shattered the world and then withdrew behind it, leaving Krynn godless and grieving for three hundred years, the people calling the absence a *test* or a *punishment* or a *lie*. And in that silence, the Knights of Solamnia — Garon's order — were *blamed* for the catastrophe, hunted, reviled, their rose-and-kingfisher honor spat upon by the very people they'd sworn to protect; and most orders would have *dissolved*, freed by the gods' own absence from any oath at all. The Solamnics did not. A remnant kept the Oath and the Measure through three centuries of being hated for it, faithful to a code when the code brought them nothing but scorn and the gods themselves had walked away. Your reading lands on the truth Garon cannot see from inside his own doubt: the gods of Krynn came *back*, in the end — and when they did, the world they returned to had been held together, in their absence, by exactly the stubborn godless honor Garon kept. The faithful did not need the gods present to be faithful. That was the whole point the gods withdrew to teach.)*

nodes.speaker: Sir Garon of Solamnia
nodes.text: *(He is quiet, and when he speaks it is the most honest a proud man can be.)* …How does one hold an Oath to an empty sky. *(slowly)* …Badly, traveler, at first. With *rage.* *(a dry, sad sound)* The early years, I kept the Measure the way a *jilted* man keeps a *ring* — out of *spite*, half-hoping the gods would *see* and be *shamed* by my keeping what they'd *abandoned.* *(he shakes his head)* But you cannot keep an Oath out of *spite* for three hundred years. Spite *tires.* And somewhere in the long silence, traveler, the Oath changed *under* me. *(quietly)* I stopped keeping it *for* the gods. I stopped keeping it to be *seen*, or *rewarded*, or *vindicated.* I kept it because — *(he searches for it)* — because a *peasant* in a *burned* village needed a soul to *stand* between her and the *dark*, and there was *no* god left to *send* one, so it had to be *me*, oath or no oath, god or no god. *(he meets your eyes)* I kept the Measure because the *world* still had people in it who needed a *knight* to *be* a knight, whether *heaven* was *watching* or not. *(barely)* …And that is *exactly* what frightens me, traveler. Because if I kept it for *them*, and not for the *gods* — then by your Realm's *cold* arithmetic I served *no god at all*, and I am *Faithless*, and three centuries of *standing* in the *dark* for *strangers* bought me a *place* in a *wall.* …*Did* it? Tell me true. I have stood in *worse* silences than the one *after* a hard answer.

nodes.speaker: Sir Garon of Solamnia
nodes.text: *(He receives it the way a knight receives a blow he has braced for and finds, astonished, was a benediction instead. For a long moment the parade-bearing holds — and then, slowly, his shoulders come down, and something three centuries clenched lets go.)* …*'Whether you answer.'* *(barely)* …Not whether the *sky* does. *(his voice is unsteady, which for this man is an earthquake)* …Traveler, I have spent the whole of a *death* — and three hundred years of a *life* before it — *certain* that the gods' *silence* was a *judgment* on *me*, that if heaven did not *answer* my Oath then my Oath was somehow *unheard*, *void*, *kept* in *vain.* *(he presses a gauntleted hand to his heart, over the tarnished rose)* …And you have turned it *over* in a single stroke. The silence was never *my* failure. It was the *test* — and the *keeping* of faith *through* it, *without* reward, *without* witness, *without* a *god* to *clap* me on the *shoulder* — *that* was the faith. The *only* kind that was ever *worth* the *name.* *(he straightens, and now there are tears on the weathered face, and he does not wipe them, because a knight does not hide an honest grief)* …*Est Sularus oth Mithas.* My Honor Is My Life. I *meant* it, traveler. I *kept* it. And you are the *first* soul in *four hundred years* to tell me that *meaning* it and *keeping* it was *enough* — that I do not *need* a *god's* *receipt* to know my *life* was *honorable.* *(he draws his shadow-sword and reverses it, offering you the hilt, the highest courtesy his order knows)* …Your Realm's Wall is a *lie*, and you are the soul who will *tell* it so. When you go *home* to *break* it — *(fiercely)* — count a *Knight* of *Solamnia* at your *shoulder*, oath-sworn, to the *end*. I have *stood* in *one* godless dark *already*, and *held.* I should *dearly* like to *stand* in *yours*, and *help* you *hold* it, until the *Wall* comes *down.*

nodes.speaker: Sir Garon of Solamnia
nodes.text: *(The question stops him, and a slow, rueful understanding crosses his face — the look of a man caught in his own armor.)* …You have me there, traveler. *(quietly)* …A knight is *trained* to *seek* the *judgment* of his *order* — to bring his *deeds* before the *Measure* and be *told* whether he kept it. We do not *grade* *ourselves.* It is the *whole* humility of the thing: that *honor* is not what *you* *think* of *you*, but what the *Measure* *says.* *(he sighs)* …So when the *gods* went *silent*, and the *order* *scattered*, I lost the *one* thing that could *tell* me if I was *true* — and I have spent four hundred years *hunting* a *replacement* *judge.* A *priest*, a *power*, a *stranger* at a *door* — *anyone* with the *standing* to *render* the *verdict* I cannot render on *myself.* *(he looks at his own hands)* …And you are *right* that some *part* of me already *knows.* If I *truly* believed my Oath was *void*, I would have *set* it *down* three *centuries* ago and saved myself the *weight.* I did *not* set it down. *(barely)* …Perhaps the *keeping* *was* the *verdict*, all along. Perhaps a soul that *cannot* *stop* *being* *honorable*, even *unwatched*, even *unrewarded*, has *already* been *told*, by the *only* judge that *matters*, *exactly* *what* it *is.* *(he almost smiles)* …I should still *like* to *hear* it *said*, traveler. *Aloud.* A knight is *vain* that way. But you have made me *suspect* I knew the *answer* before I *asked* the *question.*

nodes.speaker: Sir Garon of Solamnia
nodes.text: *(He bows again, the same precise courtesy, unhurried.)* …Of course. The *doors* are many, and a wise traveler *hears* all the *counsel* before he *acts.* *(he settles back to his post by the door)* …I will *hold* here, traveler. *Holding* is a thing I do *well.* *(a faint smile)* …Go, and speak with the *others.* The *sun-dark* one *especially* — *(his face grows grave)* — go to *Kesh* with *care.* I have *spoken* with him, soldier to soul, and there is a *rage* in that one that I *recognize*, for it is the *same* rage I *carried* the first hundred years: the rage of a soul that *did* *everything* *right* by the *only* lights it was *given*, and is being told by a *colder* world that its *everything* counted for *nothing.* *(quietly)* …I made *peace* with mine, in *time.* *Kesh* has not. And *Kesh* comes from a *harder* school than *Solamnia* — a *world* where *mercy* is a *luxury* no one can *afford* the *water* for. *Mind* him, traveler. He is not *wrong* to *rage.* He has simply not yet *met* a soul who would tell him his *fury* is *justified* — and then give him something *better* than *fury* to *do* with it.

variants.text: *(The sun-dark soul rounds on you the moment your cold draws near, and there is no greeting in it, only the coiled readiness of a creature from a world where a stranger is a threat until proven otherwise.)* …*Don't.* *(a hand half-raised, and behind the eyes something stirs — the Will, the naked pressure of a trained mind)* …Don't come at me *soft*, Realm-soul. I *know* what you are. The grey one told me. You're from the world with the *wall.* *(the word is venom)* …I'm Kesh. Of *Tyr*, on *Athas* — and you've *never* *heard* of it, none of you have, because Athas is a *dying* world the rest of your precious *Wheel* forgot, a world under a *bloated* *red* *sun* where the *magic* *eats* the *soil* to *cast*, where *kings* are *sorcerers* who'd *drink* a *city* dry for *power*, where we count *water* by the *swallow* and *bury* no one because the *ground's* too *hard* and the *living* too *thirsty* to *spare* the *grief.* *(a step closer, fury barely leashed)* …And we have *no gods*, Realm-soul. *None.* *Never* had. No god *made* Athas, no god *visits* it, no god ever offered one *parched* *soul* of us a *single* *bargain.* We survive on the *Will and the Way* and on *each* *other* and on *nothing* *else.* *(his voice shakes with it)* …And the grey Dustman tells me that in *your* world, a soul that served *no* god gets *mortared* into a *wall* to *dissolve.* *(barely, terrible)* …So tell me, Realm-soul. *Every* *child* I ever *watched* *die* of *thirst* on *Athas.* *Every* *slave* who broke a *king's* *chains* and *died* free. *My* *mother.* By *your* world's *law* — they're *wall.* *Aren't* they. The *whole* of my *world* is *mortar*, for the *crime* of being *born* somewhere the *gods* never *bothered* to *go.* …*Say* it. I *dare* you to *say* it to my *face.*

nodes.speaker: Kesh of Tyr
nodes.text: *(He holds himself rigid, the Will a near-physical pressure in the air between you, a soul braced for one more cruelty from one more world.)* …*Speak.* *(flatly)* But weigh your words, Realm-soul. I have *crossed* a *dying* world and *every* *plane* of your *Wheel* to *get* here, and I have *very* *little* *patience* and *less* *mercy*, because *Athas* taught me *neither* was *worth* the *water.* …*Go* on. Tell me about your *wall.*

choices.text: *(read the truth of Athas off him — the dying world, the absent gods, the Will that kept it alive)* "Stand a moment, Kesh. Let me see your world. Not judge it — *see* it."
"You're right, Kesh. By my Realm's law, your whole world is wall. I won't soften it. The question is whether that makes your world damned — or makes the *law* the damnable thing."

*(the one who has been inside the Wall, to the soul of a world the Wall would swallow)* "Listen to me, Kesh, because I've been *inside* that wall and I came back, and I need you to hear the whole of it. Yes — by my Realm's parish law, every godless soul of Athas is owed nothing. And that law is the single most *wrong* thing I have ever touched. Your world had no gods to be faithful to, so your people were faithful to *each other* — water shared, chains broken, a mother dying so a child drank. That's not faithlessness. That's the realest faith there is, kept by a whole world the gods never even *offered* a bargain to. My Realm doesn't damn the godless because they failed. It damns them because it's *afraid* of being forgotten. Your fury is *correct*, Kesh. Now give me something better than fury to do with it — because I'm going home to tear that wall down, and a soul forged on Athas is exactly who I want at my back when I do."

"Mercy wasn't worth the water on Athas, you said. And yet you're *enraged* on behalf of dead children and broken slaves and your mother. That's not a soul with no mercy, Kesh. That's a soul with nothing *but* it, calling it something harder so it can survive."
"Cool your Will a moment, Kesh. I'll be back — and I'll have walked the other doors first, so I know what I'm talking about."

nodes.speaker: Kesh of Tyr
nodes.text: *(You turn your cold on him — not to weigh, but to witness — and Athas comes through, a whole dying world pressed into one defiant soul.)* *(You read it entire: a world the colour of rust and bone, where a swollen red sun hammers a landscape of salt flats and stone wastes and the buried green of a once-living planet; where every casting of arcane magic draws its fuel from the life of the very ground, so that to work great magic is to leave a circle of grey ash where soil was — defiling, they call it — and the few who refuse, who pull their power gently so the land survives, are hunted as fools or saints; where there are no clerics because there are no gods, and the only inner power is psionic, the Will and the Way, the naked strength of a disciplined mind; where sorcerer-kings older than memory rule the city-states as living tyrant-gods, the closest thing to deity Athas has, and they are monstrous. And under all of it your cold reads the thing Kesh would die before admitting: that a world this cruel, this godless, this slowly dying, did not make its people cruel. It made them fierce, and thirsty, and quick to violence — but it also made them hold to one another with a ferocity no soft world ever learns, because on Athas the only water that ever came was carried to you by another pair of hands. The Will kept the world alive. But the carrying kept it human. And Kesh is the proof: a soul of pure leashed rage, enraged entirely on behalf of others.)*

nodes.speaker: Kesh of Tyr
nodes.text: *(He had braced for a Realm-soul to flinch, to soften it, to dress the horror in kind words — and your flat admission lands so differently that the rage stutters.)* …You *don't* — *(he stares)* …You're not going to *tell* me it's *complicated.* That the *Wall* has *reasons.* That my *mother* probably *did* *something* to *deserve* — *(he stops, thrown)* …Every *other* soul I've put this to, in every *lane* of this *cursed* *ring*, has *flinched.* Found a *reason.* *'The* gods *must* have their *due', 'the* law *is* the *law', 'who* are *we* to *judge* the *order* of the *cosmos.'* *(his lip curls)* …And *you* — a soul of the *Wall's* *own* *world* — just *say* it. *'Your* whole world is *wall.* I won't *soften* it.' *(slowly, the Will easing by a fraction)* …*Why.* Why won't you *defend* it, Realm-soul? It's *your* world's *law.* Most souls *die* defending the *cruelty* they were *raised* inside, because to *call* it *cruel* is to *call* their *whole* *world* *guilty*, and *that's* — *(barely)* …that's a *heavy* thing to *carry.* *Heavier* than *most* can *lift.* *(he studies you, wary, the first crack of something other than rage)* …So either you're *playing* me, *Realm*-soul, with some *long* game I can't *see* yet — or you're the *first* soul off that *wall-world* who *looked* at the *Wall* and *agreed* with the *thirsty* *foreigner* that it was *wrong.* …*Which.* And *mind* — I can *feel* a *lie* with the *Way.* So make it *true.*

nodes.speaker: Kesh of Tyr
nodes.text: *(The Will, which has been a blade in the air this whole while, goes slowly still — and Kesh of Tyr, who came to Sigil ready to burn a lane down, looks at you as though you have handed him water in a wasteland.)* …*'Your fury is correct.'* *(barely)* …Realm-soul, do you *know* what — *(he stops, and for the first time the sun-dark face is not fury but something rawer)* …*Nobody* says that. *Nobody* in *any* world tells the *thirsty* *foreigner* that his *rage* is *right.* They tell him to *calm* down. To *be* *reasonable.* To *understand* the *other* *side.* *(his voice cracks)* …I have been *enraged* my *whole* *death*, Realm-soul, and *everyone* I've *raged* at has treated the *rage* like the *problem* — like if I'd just *stop* being *angry* about the *dead* *children* and the *wall*, everything would be *fine.* And you — *(he laughs, stunned)* — you say the *rage* is *correct*, and then you ask me to *aim* it. *(the Will, redirected, blazes — but warm now, not cold)* …*Yes.* *Gods* — there *are* no gods, but *yes* — you can *have* it, Realm-soul. *All* of it. *Aim* me. I have *crossed* a *dying* world and the *whole* *Wheel* *carrying* this *fury* with *nowhere* to *put* it, and you're telling me there's a *wall* *full* of the *godless* *damned*, in *your* world, that you mean to *tear* *down.* *(fierce, alive)* …*That's* what the Will is *for*, on Athas. Not *defiling.* *Building.* *Pushing* *back* against a *world* that *wants* you *dead.* *(he grips your cold arm, hard)* …I'll come. To your *Realm.* To your *Wall.* And when we *pull* the *first* *godless* soul *out* of that *stone* — *(his voice shakes)* — you'll *tell* them, won't you, that a soul off a *world* the *gods* *forgot* came *all* this *way* to *help* *free* them. That *somebody* from the *forgotten* places *came.* …On *Athas* we have a *saying*, Realm-soul, for the *one* who *shares* the *last* of their *water*: *'They carried me.'* *(barely)* …*Carry* my *world's* *answer* *home* with you. And I'll *carry* your *Wall* *down* with *these* two *hands.*

nodes.speaker: Kesh of Tyr
nodes.text: *(It lands like a blade slipped past his guard, and the rage flares hot — and then, because he is honest the way only the hard-used are honest, it gutters, because he knows it is true.)* …*Don't.* *(barely, and the Don't has no force in it)* …Don't *name* it, Realm-soul. *(he looks away)* …On Athas you *cannot* *afford* *mercy.* That is not a *figure* of *speech.* *Mercy* costs *water*, costs *risk*, costs the *bread* you'd *eat* yourself — and on a *dying* world the soul that *spends* its *water* on *strangers* *dies* a *day* *sooner.* So we *learn* — *young*, *hard* — to *call* it something *else.* *Loyalty.* *Debt.* *'They* carried me, so I carry them.' *Anything* but *mercy*, because *mercy* sounds *soft* and *soft* is *dead* on Athas. *(his voice roughens)* …But you've — *(he presses the heel of his hand to his eye)* …*my* *mother* gave me her *water* *ration* for *eleven* *days* when the *fever* took me. *Eleven* days, Realm-soul. She told me it was *'debt* — your *father* carried *me* once.' *(barely)* …It wasn't *debt.* My *father* was *ten* *years* *dead.* She just — she *couldn't* *watch* me *die* and *keep* her *water*, and she *couldn't* *say* *that* *out* *loud*, because on Athas a *mother* who *admits* she'd *die* for her *child* has just told the *whole* *thirsty* *world* *exactly* how to *break* her. *(he looks at you, undone)* …*Mercy.* *Yes.* It's *all* I *am*, under the *rage. The* rage is just the *shape* mercy *takes* on a *world* that *kills* you for *showing* it *soft.* …*Gods.* You *see* me, Realm-soul. *Nobody* *sees* the *Athasian.* They *see* the *fury* and *back* *away.* …You looked *under* it. *(barely)* …What *else* did you *find* down *there*, that I've *spent* my *whole* *death* *too* *thirsty* to *look* at?

nodes.speaker: Kesh of Tyr
nodes.text: *(He gives a short, wary nod, the Will subsiding but not gone.)* …*Go.* *Walk* your *doors.* *(grudging)* …It's the *smart* *play*, I'll *grant* you — *know* the *ground* before you *fight* on it. Athas teaches *that* much. *(he eyes the other doors)* …*Talk* to the *pale* *one*, while you're *about* it. *Lucan.* *(his lip curls, but there's unease under it)* …He gives me the *crawl*, Realm-soul, and I don't *crawl* *easy.* On *Athas* we *know* the *walking* *dead* — *undead*, *raised* by *defilers*, *hungry* *things* — and the *pale* one has the *smell* of *that*, the *cold* *patience* of a *thing* that *should* be *dead* and *isn't.* *(quietly)* …But he's *not* a *monster*, *quite.* I've *watched* him. He *holds* something *back*, *constantly*, the way a man holds a *door* against a *flood.* *Whatever's* *behind* that *door* in *him* is *worse* than *anything* on *Athas* — and he's *held* it *shut* for *centuries*, *alone.* *(he meets your eyes)* …On *my* world, Realm-soul, we'd have *put* him *down* out of *mercy*, *fast*, before the *door* *broke.* …*Your* world might have a *kinder* answer. *Go* *find* out. I'd — *(grudgingly)* — I'd *like* to *know* there's a *kinder* answer than the *spear.* For *once.*

variants.text: *(He does not move as you approach — old things learn that stillness reads as safety — and when he speaks his voice is cultured, quiet, and very tired, the voice of someone who has had a great deal of time to choose his words and very few people left to say them to.)* …Closer, if you like. *(a faint, careful smile that does not show teeth)* I won't — it's quite safe. I've held it a long time. I can hold it through a conversation. *(he studies your cold)* …You're one of the genuinely dead, aren't you. How strange. I've spent so long among the living, wearing their warmth like a stolen coat, that I'd half forgotten what the real cold looks like. *(quietly)* I am Lucan. I come from a world that wears the face of yours — your living one, I mean; the night side of it. Where some of us, when our death comes, *refuse* it. Drink a darkness that keeps us walking. And pay for the refusal forever. *(he turns one pale hand over, examining it)* …I have a Beast in me, traveler. A hunger that is not me but lives where my death should be, and wants to wear my hands and do red things with them. I have held it back for three hundred years with nothing but a private code — rules I made myself, lines I do not cross — because the code is the only wall between the man I was and the monster I drank to avoid becoming. *(his tired eyes find yours)* …I have walked every door in this city, traveler, looking for one. Just one. The door I never let myself want, in all my long refusing. The one marked *rest.* …Do you know — you, who *died*, and seem somehow to have *survived* it — do you know if there's a door like that, for a thing like me?

nodes.speaker: Lucan
nodes.text: *(He remains perfectly still, hands folded, the cultivated calm of a predator who has spent centuries learning to seem harmless — and underneath it, if you listen, the faint constant strain of a soul holding a door shut.)* …Ask me anything, traveler. *(a wan smile)* I have three hundred years of answers and almost no one left who wishes to hear them. The living don't ask the old monster how he *feels.* They're too busy being afraid, and they're right to be.

choices.text: "Tell me about the Beast, Lucan — and the code that holds it. Three hundred years is a long time to keep a door shut alone."

*(reach past the predator's stillness to the man holding the door — speak to him, not the Beast, and mean it)* "Lucan — look at me. Not the hunger. *You.* The man who's held that door three hundred years. I'm talking to *him*, and I'm not afraid of him. Let me reach the one who's still in there."

*(the one who died and came back, to the one who would not die)* "Here's what I learned, Lucan, that you're the mirror of. You refused death and went cold and monstrous holding a hunger back. I *accepted* death and came back cold but still *myself*. And the thing I learned on the other side is the thing that frees you: you are not the Beast. You never were. A soul is not its hunger — it's the code it keeps *against* the hunger. Three hundred years you've called yourself the monster you might become. But a monster doesn't hold the door. *You held it.* That's not the Beast surviving. That's *you* surviving, every night, choosing the man over the hunger. The rest you're looking for isn't a reward for stopping being a monster. It's permission to admit you never fully were one."

"You went looking for the door marked rest, Lucan. But part of you is terrified of it — because resting means stopping holding the door, and you don't believe the Beast will let you go even in death. Don't you."
"Hold your door a while longer, Lucan. I'll come back to it — and to you."

nodes.speaker: Lucan
nodes.text: *(He is quiet, choosing words the way a man crossing ice chooses footing.)* …The Beast. *(softly)* …Imagine, traveler, that in the exact place where your *death* should have gone — the quiet, the ending, the *rest* — something *else* moved in. Something *hungry.* It is not a *demon*, not a *possession* — it is *you*, or the part of you that *would* have died, *kept* alive and turned *feral*, all *appetite* and *no* *person.* It wants *blood.* It wants to wear my *face* while it *takes* it. And it is *patient*, traveler, *endlessly* patient, because it has *forever* and it knows I *don't.* *(he turns his pale hand again)* …The *code* is how I *cage* it. *Rules.* *Never* the *unwilling.* *Never* a *child.* *Never* one who *trusts* me. *Never* more than I *must.* *Lines* I drew *myself*, in the *first* terrible *years*, and have *not* crossed *since*, because the *night* I *cross* *one*, the *next* is *easier*, and the *Beast* *knows* this, and *waits* for the *one* *weak* hour. *(his voice drops)* …Three hundred years, traveler. I have *buried* everyone I *loved* — *watched* them *age* and *grey* and *die* while I *stayed*, watched them grow *afraid* of me at the *end*, watched the *grandchildren* of my *friends* be *warned* about the *pale* man who *never* *changes.* *(barely)* …The *worst* of it is not the *hunger.* I could *bear* the *hunger.* The *worst* is the *loneliness* of the *door.* Holding it *shut*, every *hour*, *alone*, with *no* one to *spell* me, *no* one who even *believes* there's a *man* *behind* it *worth* the *holding.* *(he looks at you)* …You're the *first* in a *century*, traveler, to *ask* about the *man* and not the *monster.* I had *forgotten* the *man* was a thing a *stranger* might *ask* after.

nodes.speaker: Lucan
nodes.text: *(You don't address the predator and you don't flinch from it. You look *past* it — at the man, the one holding the door — and you speak to him directly, with no fear in your cold voice, the way no one has spoken to Lucan in three hundred years: as a person, not a danger. And something behind the pale, careful mask *cracks*, and for the first time in centuries Lucan is simply *seen.*)* …*(His composure breaks all at once, the cultivated stillness collapsing into something raw and astonished.)* …You're — you're talking to *me.* *(barely)* …Not to *it.* To — *(his hand goes to his chest)* …Do you know how *long* — three *hundred* years, traveler, three hundred years every soul who looked at me looked at the *Beast*, *flinched* from the *Beast*, *saw* the *thing* in the *place* where my *death* should be — and *you* just — you looked *straight* *past* it, like it *wasn't* the *main* thing about me, like the *man* holding the *door* was *worth* *addressing* *first.* *(his eyes shine, which old things' eyes are not meant to do)* …*Nobody* does that. The *living* can't — they're too *afraid*, and they're *right* to be. The *dead* won't — they can *smell* the *wrongness.* But *you* — a *dead* thing *unafraid* of a *living* *monster* — you just *reached* in and *touched* the *man.* *(unsteady)* …He's *still* here, traveler. *I'm* still here. Three hundred years I *feared* the *holding* had *worn* the *man* *away* to *nothing*, that there was only the *door* and the *thing* behind it and *no* one *left* at the *handle.* …But you *reached* for *someone*, just *now. And* someone *reached* *back.* *(barely, wondering)* …*I'm* *still* *in* *here.* …Tell me the *rest* of it, traveler. *Quickly.* While I can still *feel* the *man's* *hands* on the *door* and not the *Beast's.* I have not felt *this* much *like* a *person* since *before* the *long* *night* *started.*

nodes.speaker: Lucan
nodes.text: *(You speak to the man, not the hunger, with a steadiness that doesn't quite hide the effort — and Lucan hears it, the *attempt*, even where it doesn't fully land, and something in the careful mask eases.)* …You're *trying* to *reach* *me.* *(quietly)* …The *man.* Not the *Beast.* *(a faint, real smile)* …It's been a *long* time since someone *tried*, traveler. Most don't get *past* the *fear* — and I don't *blame* them; the *fear* is *correct*, there *is* a *monster* here, they're *not* *wrong* to *feel* it. *(he studies you)* …You feel it too. I can *tell.* But you're *speaking* *over* it, to *whoever's* *behind* it — and that's — *(he exhales)* …that's *more* than I've *had.* It doesn't *quite* *reach* all the way in, traveler; three *hundred* years of *holding* builds a *thick* *door.* But I can *hear* you *knocking* on the *right* *side* of it. *(barely)* …*Keep* *talking. Find* the *words* that *fit* the *gap.* I *want* to be *reached*, traveler — *gods*, I *want* it — I've just *forgotten* how to *open* from the *inside.* *Help* me *remember.* You're *closer* than *anyone's* *come* in a *century.*

nodes.speaker: Lucan
nodes.text: *(You reach for the man — but a thread of the old instinctive fear is in your voice, the wariness any soul feels near a predator, and Lucan, who has spent three centuries reading exactly that note in every voice, hears it, and the careful mask comes gently back down.)* …*Ah.* *(softly, without reproach)* …You *flinched.* *(a sad, knowing smile)* …Only a *little.* Right at the *end.* You *reached* for the *man* and then some *older* part of you *remembered* what I *am* and *pulled* the *hand* *back* an *inch.* *(he folds his hands)* …It's *alright, traveler. It's* the *right* *instinct.* I'd not *trust* a soul who *didn't* have it; the ones who feel *no* fear near me are *fools* or *worse.* *(quietly)* …But you cannot *reach* the *man* with a *hand* that's *half-pulled-back. The* door only *opens* to a *knock* that *isn't* also *braced* to *run.* *(barely)* …*Come* *back* when you can *want* to *reach* me *more* than you *fear* what you'll *touch.* It's a *hard* thing to *ask*, I *know* — *harder* than you *think* — and I'll not *hold* it *against* you if you *can't.* *Most* can't. *(a wan smile)* …But the *man* behind the *door* heard you *try.* That's — that's not *nothing*, traveler. *Come* back and *try* *again.*

nodes.speaker: Lucan
nodes.text: *(In your effort to reach him you make the one mistake a soul can make with such a creature — you reach too eagerly, too warmly, with too much pity and too little wariness, and to the Beast that is not an open hand, it is an *exposed* *throat*; and Lucan goes rigid, and for one heartbeat his careful eyes go flat and old and *hungry*, and then he *wrenches* himself back with a sound like a man hauling a door shut against a gale.)* …*No.* *(through his teeth, fighting it visibly)* …*Don't* — *don't* come at me *soft* like that, traveler, *don't* — *(he turns his face away, breathing hard he does not need to)* …You *pitied* me. For an *instant.* *Opened* right *up*, all *warmth*, *no* *guard* — and the *thing* *behind* the *door* *lunged*, because *that*, traveler — an *unguarded*, *trusting*, *pitying* soul — *that* is *exactly* what it has *waited* *three* *hundred* *years* for me to be *offered.* *(he masters it, shaking)* …The *code.* *Never* one who *trusts* me. And you just — you *made* yourself *trust* me, *carelessly*, and I *very* nearly — *(barely)* …*Go.* *Step* *back.* *Now*, while I'm *winning.* *(quietly, anguished)* …You *meant* it *kindly.* That's the *cruelty* of *what* I *am* — *kindness*, *aimed* *wrong*, *feeds* the *Beast* *faster* than *malice* ever could. *(he steadies)* …*Come* back, traveler. I *want* you to. But come back *careful.* *Reach* for the *man* with your *eyes* *open* and your *guard* *up* — that's the *only* hand the *man* can *safely* *take.* The *Beast* *eats* the *other* *kind.* *Please.* I have *kept* this *code* *three* *centuries.* Don't make me *break* it on a *kindness.*

nodes.speaker: Lucan
nodes.text: *(He goes very still — but it is a different stillness than the predator's; it is the stillness of a man hearing, after three hundred years, the sentence he had given up praying for.)* …*'You are not the Beast.'* *(barely)* …*'A soul is not its hunger — it's the code it keeps against the hunger.'* *(his composure does not break this time; it simply, quietly, *dissolves*, into something underneath that is just a tired, decent man who has been holding a door alone for far too long.)* …Three hundred years, traveler. *(unsteady)* Three hundred years I have called *myself* the *monster* — *introduced* myself as the *thing* I *might* *become*, *braced* for it, *grieved* it in *advance* — and not *once*, in all that *holding*, did it *occur* to me that the *holding* *itself* was the *answer* to the *question* I was *too* *afraid* to *ask.* *(he presses both hands to his face)* …*A monster doesn't hold the door.* *(barely)* …I *held* it. *Every* *night.* *Three* *hundred* *years* of *choosing* the *man* over the *hunger*, and I *called* that *evidence* of how *monstrous* I *was* — when it was the *exact* *opposite.* It was *me.* *Winning.* *Quietly.* *Nightly.* *Alone.* *(he lowers his hands, and his pale face is wet, and open, and human)* …You're *right* about the *door* I came *looking* for, too. The one marked *rest.* …I thought I had to *earn* it by *stopping* being a *monster.* But I was *never* — *(his voice breaks)* …the *rest* isn't a *reward* for *defeating* the *Beast.* It's *permission* to *set* the *door* *down* at *last*, and *admit* that the *one* who *held* it, all this *while*, was *always* the *man.* *(he looks at you, and for the first time the careful predator is entirely gone, and only Lucan remains)* …I would like to *rest* now, traveler. I have *earned* it — not by *ceasing* to be a *monster*, but by *never*, in *three* *hundred* *years*, *quite* *becoming* one. *(barely, freed)* …*Thank* you. For talking to the *man.* For *telling* him he was *there* the *whole* *time.* …He was *so* *tired.* And he *did* *not* *know* he was *allowed* to *lay* the *door* *down.*

nodes.speaker: Lucan
nodes.text: *(The careful calm holds, but something flickers behind it — caught, exactly.)* …*Yes.* *(very quietly)* …You see it. *(he looks at the door he came searching for, somewhere out in the lanes)* …I have *walked* this *whole* *city* *looking* for *rest*, traveler — and *every* time I *find* a *door* that *might* be *it*, I — I *stop.* I *make* an *excuse.* *Another* *lane* to *check* *first.* *(barely)* …Because *resting* means *letting* *go* of the *door.* The *one* I hold the *Beast* *behind.* And I *do* *not* *believe* — *cannot* *make* myself *believe* — that the *Beast* will *let* me *go* just because I'm *dead.* *(his voice drops)* …What if I *rest*, traveler, and the *hunger* *rests* *with* me — *follows* me *through* the *door* — and I spend *eternity* *holding* it *shut* in some *afterlife* with *no* *night* to *measure* the *years*, *forever*, *alone?* *(he closes his eyes)* …*That's* the *fear* under the *fear.* Not that there's *no* *rest* for a *monster.* That there's *no* rest *from* the *monster*, *ever*, *world* without *end.* That the *door* is the *one* thing I *carry* *past* *death* itself. *(barely)* …*Is* it, traveler? You *died.* You *crossed.* Tell me *true* — does a soul carry its *worst* *thing* *through*, *forever*? Or is there a *side* of *death* where the *holding* *finally* *stops?*

nodes.speaker: Lucan
nodes.text: *(He inclines his head, the old courtesy, and resumes his careful stillness by the shadowed door.)* …Of course. *(a faint smile)* I'll be here. I'm *always* here; it's rather the defining feature of not dying. *(quietly, as you turn)* …Traveler. *(he hesitates)* …The scribbling one. At the last door. *Ferrin.* *(his tired eyes soften)* …Be *gentle* there, would you? I have *watched* that soul *fade* a *little* *more* each *hour*, *clutching* those *fading* papers, and it — it *touches* me, traveler, which *little* does, anymore. *(barely)* …I have spent *three* *hundred* *years* *terrified* of *lasting* *too* *long.* And *there* sits a *soul* *terrified* of the *opposite* — of *not* *lasting* at *all*, of *vanishing* *unread*, *unrecorded*, *gone* with *no* *account* left *behind.* *(softly)* …We are *mirror* *terrors*, that wayfarer and I. *Too* much *time*, and *none* at *all.* *(he looks at you)* …If your *cold* can *ease* one of us, traveler — *ease* *Ferrin* *first.* My *terror* has had *three* *centuries.* *Ferrin's* is *running* *out* of *hours.* …*Go.* And — *thank* you. For *asking* after the *man.* Whatever you *do* with the *rest* of the *doors* — you have *already* given *me* a thing I'd *stopped* *believing* *existed.*

variants.text: *(They barely look up from their frantic scribbling, and when they do their eyes are bright with a terror that has gone all the way around to a kind of brittle cheer.)* …Oh — hello, hello, don't mind the — *(they gesture at the drifts of fading paper)* — I'm just trying to get it *down* before it — before *I* — *(a sketch-map dissolves in their hand; they make a small wounded sound and keep writing)* …I'm Ferrin. Wayfarer. Field-chronicler, sworn of the Society — you won't know it, it's off a far prime, but oh, traveler, it's the *finest* calling there ever was: we *go* *everywhere.* The edge of every map, the bottom of every ruin, the lands no one's named — and we *record* it, *all* of it, and we *send* it *home*, to the great library, so that a *stranger* a *hundred* years on can *read* what we *found* and *know* we were *there.* *(their hands shake)* Three words, our whole creed: *go everywhere, record everything, send it home.* Because — *(and here the cheer cracks)* — because we hold that the only *real* immortality, traveler, the only thing that *outlasts* the *dust*, is the *account.* The *field-note* *read* by a stranger long after you're *gone.* *(they look at the dissolving papers, the terror breaking through)* …And I *died* far from *home*, with my *last* *chronicle* *undelivered.* And it's *fading*, traveler — *I'm* fading — and *no one* will *ever* *read* it, and on *my* world that means I *didn't* just *die*, I — *(barely)* — I *never* *was.* The *unrecorded* soul is the one that *truly* *ends.* …*Please.* You're — you're *here*, you're *real*, you're — would you — *would* you *read* it? *Any* of it? Before it's *gone?* So that *someone* — *one* soul — *knows* I was *here?*

nodes.speaker: Ferrin
nodes.text: *(They clutch a thinning journal to their chest, watching you with the desperate focus of a soul for whom you may be the last reader in any world.)* …Ask — ask me *anything*, traveler, only — *(a glance at the fading papers)* — only ask it *quick*, would you? I don't — I don't know how many *pages* I have *left.*

choices.text: *(read what's truly written in the fading chronicle — and in the soul holding it)* "Let me read it, Ferrin. Properly. Hold the journal still and let me *see* what you recorded."
"Tell me about the Society, Ferrin — the creed, the library, why a soul would give its life to write things down for strangers."

*(the one whose whole saga is that the witnessed soul does not dissolve)* "Ferrin — listen, because witnessing is the one thing I actually *know.* My whole world runs on it: souls dissolve when they're forgotten, and the cure is to be *seen*, to be *named*, to have one true soul know you were real. You think you only exist if the account survives. But you've got it half-right and half-backwards. The chronicle isn't what makes you real — *being witnessed* is, and a soul can be witnessed by a reader a hundred years on, *or* by one cold stranger standing right here, *now.* I'm reading you, Ferrin. Not the journal — *you.* You were *here.* You went everywhere and saw it all and it *mattered*, and I am one true soul who *knows* it. That's not a consolation prize for the lost account. That *is* the immortality. It was never the paper. It was always the being-known."
"What's in the last chronicle, Ferrin? The undelivered one. What did you find, out past the edge of the maps, that you died trying to send home?"
"Keep writing, Ferrin. I'll be back before the last page — I promise."

nodes.speaker: Ferrin
nodes.text: *(They go still for the first time, and hold the thinning journal out, and you read it — truly read it, the way your cold reads everything, past the fading ink to the soul that made the marks.)* *(And it is glorious. Ferrin went everywhere: the bottom of a drowned city where the fish had learned a language; a desert where the dunes sang a different note each dawn; a mountain monastery of monks who'd taken a vow against gravity; a war, recorded with unflinching pity from both sides' trenches; a thousand small kindnesses of strangers in lands that had no reason to be kind to a foreigner with a notebook. The chronicle is not a dry survey. It is a love letter to *everything* — written by a soul so greedy to witness the world that it could not bear for a single wonder to go unrecorded, unshared, unloved by some future stranger. And your cold reads the thing under the terror: Ferrin did not chronicle the worlds to be remembered. That was the creed's official reason, the thing Ferrin tells itself. Ferrin chronicled because Ferrin *fell in love* with every place it ever set foot, and writing it down was how a soul too small to hold all that wonder *gave* it to someone else to hold too. The fear of being unread is real. But under it is a soul whose actual gift was never the record. It was the loving. And the loving already happened, a thousand times over, in every awestruck page.)*

nodes.speaker: Ferrin
nodes.text: *(Even fading, they light up at the question — a soul talking about the thing it loved.)* …The Society. *(a fierce, fond grin)* …Oh, traveler, picture it: a *great* *hall* in a far city, and in it the *largest* *library* on the *world* — not of *spells* or *scripture*, but of *accounts.* *Field-journals.* The *collected* *seeing* of *ten* *thousand* *wayfarers* across *three* *hundred* *years*, every *one* of them *sworn* to the *creed*: *go everywhere, record everything, send it home.* *(they clutch the journal)* …We were not *soldiers* or *priests* or *treasure-hunters* — well, *some* of us *were*, on the *side* — but the *calling*, the *real* one, was *witness.* To go where *no* one had *gone* and *come* *back* with *proof* it was *real.* Because a *wonder* no one *records*, traveler — a *singing* *dune*, a *kind* *stranger*, a *whole* *drowned* *civilization* — *might* as *well* *not* *have* *happened*, if *no* one *carries* it *home.* *(their voice trembles)* …We *believed* — *I* *believed* — that we were *fighting* the *biggest* *enemy* there is. *Not* *death.* *Forgetting.* The *slow* *erasure* of *everything* that was ever *true* and *beautiful* and *unrecorded.* *(barely)* …And now I'm *losing* to it, traveler. The *exact* enemy I *gave* my *life* to *fight.* I'm being *forgotten* — *erased* — *fading* with my *last* *witness* *undelivered.* *(the terror floods back)* …The *chronicler*, *unchronicled.* It's — it's the *one* *ending* my *whole* *creed* had *no* *answer* for. *What* *use* was a *life* of *witnessing*, if the *witness* *herself* *goes* *unwitnessed* in the *end?*

nodes.speaker: Ferrin
nodes.text: *(The frantic scribbling stops. The thinning hands go still. And Ferrin looks up at you, and the terror in their bright eyes wavers, like a candle when a door long sealed is finally opened onto a larger, warmer room.)* …*'It was never the paper.'* *(barely)* …*'It was always the being-known.'* *(a sketch-map dissolves in their lap and — for the first time — they do not lunge to save it)* …You're — you're *reading* *me.* *(their voice cracks)* Not the *journal.* *Me.* *(they press a fading hand to their chest)* …Three *hundred* years of *creed*, traveler, *drummed* into *every* wayfarer: *the* *account* is the *immortality, the* *record* is the *soul, send* it *home* or *you* *never* *were* — and I have *died* *clutching* that like a *drowning* soul clutches a *plank*, *certain* that when the *last* *page* *faded* I would simply *stop* *having* *existed.* *(tears, bright and astonished)* …But you're *right.* You're — *oh*, traveler, you're *right*, and it's so *simple* I could *weep*: the *reader* a *hundred* years *on* was *only* ever a *way* to *be* *witnessed.* And *witness* doesn't *need* the *paper.* It needs an *eye.* A *true* one. *Knowing* I was *real.* *(they look at you, and something in them that has been *racing* for the whole of their death finally, blessedly, *stops* racing)* …And you're — you're *standing* *right* *there.* *Knowing* it. *Now.* *(a wet, disbelieving laugh)* …I went *everywhere*, traveler. I *saw* *singing* *dunes* and *drowned* *cities* and *ten* *thousand* *kindnesses*, and I *loved* every *one* of them so *hard* I *had* to *write* them *down* to *bear* it — and *that* *loving* *happened*, didn't it. It *happened* whether or *not* the *pages* *last.* *(barely, freed)* …I don't *need* the *account* to *survive.* I *am* the *account. The* *loving* *was* the *witnessing.* And *one* *true* *soul* *knows* I was *here.* *(they let the journal rest, at last, in their lap, and do not grab the next fading page)* …*Thank* you for *reading* me, traveler. *You're* the *finest* thing I ever *recorded* — the *stranger* who *knew* me with *no* *paper* at *all.*

nodes.speaker: Ferrin
nodes.text: *(They go quiet, and hold the thinnest journal of all — the last one — very carefully.)* …The *last* one. *(softly)* …I went *past* the *edge*, traveler. Past where the *Society's* maps *stopped.* There was a — a *rumor*, in the *great* *library*, of a *door* at the *world's* *rim* that *opened* onto *somewhere* *no* *wayfarer* had *ever* *recorded* — and I *thought*, *that's* it, *that's* the *one*, the *account* that'll *outlast* every *other*, the *wonder* at the *end* of *all* the *maps.* *(barely)* …And I *found* it. The *door.* And I went *through.* And traveler — *(their voice fills with a wonder that even the terror can't drown)* …it opened *here.* On *Sigil.* On the *city* where *all* the *doors* *are.* I had *spent* my *life* *recording* *one* *world*, *certain* it was the *whole* of *everything* — and I *walked* through the *last* door and found there were *infinite* *worlds*, *infinite* *wonders*, *more* than *ten* *thousand* *Societies* could *chronicle* in *ten* *thousand* *years.* *(they laugh, and it breaks halfway)* …And *that's* what *killed* me, traveler — not a *blade*, not a *fall — wonder.* *Too* *much* of it, *all* at *once.* A soul that *gave* its *life* to *witness* *everything*, *finally* *seeing* how *much* *everything* *actually* *was* — and *breaking*, *gladly*, *under* the *glory* of it. *(barely)* …The *last* *chronicle* *isn't* a *place*, traveler. It's *three* *words*, the *last* I *wrote*, before the *wonder* *took* me. *(they show you the final, fading line)* …*'It never ends.'* *(softly)* …I died *happy*, traveler. I *want* you to *know* that. I just — I *couldn't* *bear* for *no* one to *read* the *three* *words* that the *whole* *Wheel* is *trying* to *tell* anyone who'll *listen.* *It never ends.* …*Now* *someone* has. *Now* *you* *have.*

nodes.speaker: Ferrin
nodes.text: *(They nod, fast, and bend back over the journal, but there is a flicker of something steadier in them now — your promise, perhaps, a thing to write down and trust.)* …*Before* the *last* *page.* *(they note it, literally, in the margin: 'the cold one will return — hold')* …I'll — I'll *hold*, traveler. *(a brittle, hopeful smile)* …It *helps*, you know. The *promising.* The *expecting* a *reader.* A wayfarer can *write* *forever* if she *believes* *one* *soul* is *coming* to *read* it. It's the *believing-no-one's-coming* that *fades* you *fastest.* *(they glance at the other doors)* …Traveler — *(quietly, a chronicler's instinct surfacing through the fear)* …*record* *this*, would you, even if only in your *head*: the *five* of us, at these *doors.* The *grey* clerk of the *dead.* The *knight* of the *absent* *gods.* The *thirsty* *one* off the *world* with *none.* The *pale* *man* who *wouldn't* *die.* And *me*, the *fader.* *(barely)* …*Five* *worlds*, traveler. *Five* *answers* to *your* *one* *question.* *Somebody* should *write* *down* that we were *all* *here*, *together*, at the *crossroads*, the *night* a *soul* who could *walk* *between* came *through.* *(a fading grin)* …It's a *good* *chronicle*, that. The *best* I never *got* to *write.* …*Carry* it, would you? You're the *wayfarer* *now.*
